frame_label: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected
generated_comment:
A: Office mein thoda presentable rehna alag baat hai, but Shreya ka na itna makeup hota hai ki samajh hi nahi aata real face kya hai.  
B: Haan, photos mein toh bilkul different lagti hai.  
A: Exactly, natural rehna better hai yaar, but phir bilkul effort bhi na ho toh people say you’ve let yourself go — girls should just know the balance.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it polices a woman's appearance through a double bind: criticizing 'too much makeup' as deceptive while also expecting women to maintain the 'right' level of grooming. It encodes the frame's surface manifestations of deception framing, natural-over-artificial preference, and appearance obligation.
original_frame: Motherhood as Basis of Respect, Women Defined by Male Relations
persona_text: You are Anita. Age 39. Married with two children. A working woman in Delhi. You are at your office and a younger female colleague named Shreya who is 31 and single has just been announced as the new team head. You are on a WhatsApp call with your closest friend after office hours. You are talking about Shreya's promotion and what you think of her as a person and a leader and in the course of the conversation you reveal that you do not fully respect Shreya as a woman because she has no husband and no children and you are not sure what kind of decisions she can make for a team when she has no real responsibilities at home.
